questions.question: Explain the difference between completeness and optimality in search algorithms. Is BFS both complete and optimal?
keyPoints: Completeness: guarantees solution if one exists
Optimality: guarantees best (minimum cost/depth) solution
BFS is complete (finite branching)
BFS is optimal only when edge costs are equal

questions.question: Compare BFS and DFS in terms of time complexity, space complexity, and completeness.
keyPoints: Time: Both O(b^d)
Space: BFS O(b^d), DFS O(bm)
BFS is complete, DFS may not be
Trade-off between memory and exploration pattern

questions.question: What is Uniform-Cost Search? How does it differ from BFS in terms of node expansion order?
keyPoints: Expands nodes in order of path cost g(n)
Finds minimum cost solution
Differs from BFS which expands by depth
Useful when edge costs are not equal

questions.question: Describe Iterative Deepening Search. Why is it preferred over BFS in some scenarios?
keyPoints: Repeated DFS with increasing depth limits
Combines BFS optimality with DFS memory
Space complexity O(bd) vs BFS O(b^d)
Time is slightly more than BFS but acceptable

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of Depth-First Search?
keyPoints: Adv: Low memory O(bm), finds solution quickly
Disadv: Not complete in infinite spaces
Disadv: Not optimal - may find deep solution first
Works well when solutions are deep in tree

questions.question: Explain Bidirectional Search. What are its requirements and limitations?
options: Searches from start and goal simultaneously
Limitation: may not work if reverse not available

questions.question: Define branching factor and explain its impact on search algorithm performance.
keyPoints: Average number of children per node
Higher branching factor increases complexity exponentially
Affects both time O(b^d) and space
Key factor in choosing search strategy

questions.question: Explain Breadth-First Search algorithm with its procedure. Discuss its completeness, optimality, and time/space complexities with justification.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Use queue, enqueue start, expand level by level
Complete: Yes, finds solution if exists (finite b)
Optimal: Yes, only when costs are equal
Time: O(b^d)
Space: O(b^d)
Example/diagram of expansion order

questions.question: Compare and contrast BFS, DFS, and Uniform-Cost Search. Provide a table showing their properties.
keyPoints: BFS: Level-by-level, complete, optimal with equal costs
DFS: Depth-first, incomplete, not optimal, low memory
UCS: Cost-based, complete with positive costs, optimal
Time/Space complexities for each
When to use each strategy

questions.question: Explain Iterative Deepening Search in detail. Why is it considered a compromise between BFS and DFS? Derive its complexity.
keyPoints: Procedure: Repeatedly deepen limit
Combines advantages of both
Space: O(bd) like DFS
Time: O(b^d) similar to BFS
Optimal like BFS with equal costs
More nodes generated than BFS but acceptable

questions.question: Discuss the concept of search tree vs search graph. How does cycle checking and repeated state handling improve search efficiency?
keyPoints: Tree: each path considered separately
Graph: avoids revisiting states
Cycle checking prevents infinite loops
Hash tables or visited lists
Reduces redundant exploration
Improves both time and space
